summary: A substantial reconstruction of the ancient Land of Israel rite whose distinctive liturgical order, gender-neutral Hebrew, and recurring translation of the divine name as ‘the Cause’ make it valuable as a clearly labeled historical alternative rather than an invisible default spine.

sourceAssessment.voice: Reconstructed, scholarly, bilingual, prayerful, and textually dense, with a deliberately distinctive English translation vocabulary.
bestUses: Historically grounded alternatives for Kadesh, Maggid, ritual-symbol explanations, and Hallel
A traditional-theme sidebar on the diversity of ancient Jewish Passover rites
Long-form adult or facilitator editions that can provide adequate historical context
beginnerRisks: The translation’s use of ‘the Cause’ for the divine name will surprise readers unless it is explained first.
Long bilingual passages and reconstructed ritual variants can obscure the practical action for first-time hosts.
Some source-specific instructions presume liturgical fluency and should not replace the project’s plain-language host directions.
sourceAssessment.politicalContext: This is a historical Palestinian-Jewish liturgical reconstruction, not a statement about modern nationalism. Biblical Israel, Jerusalem, and land language must be labeled as historical or liturgical and must never be turned into an exclusive contemporary territorial claim.
sourceAssessment.creditContext: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur, identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 source, indicate any changes, and preserve the applicable ShareAlike terms. Embedded source material still requires item-level review.

recommendedSequences.title: First cup in the reconstructed rite
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Explain that this is a reconstructed ancient Land of Israel form of the first-cup blessing, then tell guests whether to read the English, Hebrew, or both.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These adjacent passages complete the first-cup blessing and its seasonal additions without jumping to a different ritual moment.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; identify the CC BY-SA 4.0 edition and any adaptation.

recommendedSequences.title: Questions leading into servitude
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce the Four Questions as observations about what makes this night different, and explain that the response begins the story of oppression and the cry for freedom.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The source moves directly from the questions into its compact account of forced labor, prayer, and deliverance, preserving the question-and-answer logic of Maggid.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; retain source attribution on the final credits page.

recommendedSequences.title: From ancestral beginnings to the plagues
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Before reading, give a short spoken bridge: the Haggadah widens the Exodus story by tracing the family’s earlier migrations, enslavement, cry, and divine response.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Five consecutive passages carry one sustained biblical exposition from ancestral history through oppression and deliverance to the naming of the ten plagues.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur and preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution and ShareAlike conditions.

recommendedSequences.title: Passover, matzah, and maror explained
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Point to each ritual food as it is named and explain that these passages connect what guests can see and taste to the remembered movement from bondage toward freedom.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The three adjacent records give the source’s complete explanation of the central symbols and then turn naturally from explanation toward gratitude and song.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; indicate any abridgment while retaining the CC BY-SA 4.0 terms.

recommendedSequences.title: Praise after the freedom story
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Tell guests that the story now shifts into poetry: the table responds to liberation with Psalms and a historical liturgical composition.
recommendedSequences.rationale: These source-adjacent passages preserve the movement from Psalm 113 into the sea imagery of Psalm 114 and the rite’s added poetic retelling.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur and retain any passage-level attributions resolved from the canonical source.

recommendedSequences.title: Matzah, meal, and table blessing
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Separate the practical actions from the source reading: first explain what everyone eats and when, then identify the transition from the meal to the third cup and grace.
recommendedSequences.rationale: The sequence follows the source’s own order from eating matzah and maror through the meal into the opening movements of blessing after food.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; adaptations remain subject to CC BY-SA 4.0.

recommendedSequences.title: Nishmat and embodied praise
recommendedSequences.beginnerContext: Introduce this as an extended closing poem in which breath, body, language, and community become instruments of gratitude; it belongs only in a spacious edition.
recommendedSequences.rationale: Eight consecutive segments preserve a complete long-form arc of Nishmat rather than extracting isolated devotional sentences from the middle of the prayer.
recommendedSequences.creditNotes: Credit Isaac Gantwerk Mayer and Open Siddur; preserve CC BY-SA 4.0 attribution, change notice, and ShareAlike requirements.

reasons: Source and reconstruction metadata should appear in credits or editorial notes, not as an unannounced liturgical reading.

notes: All recommended material remains candidate-only and requires a separate human editorial decision before runtime approval.
This source is included as a historically specific, opt-in layer; it is not excluded merely because its rite or divine-name translation differs from the default.
Do not modernize or silently normalize the translation. A bridge may explain it, but exact source wording must remain exact.
